stories.title: Kristoffer Reitan Wins 2026 Truist Championship — Norwegian Rookie Claims First PGA Tour Title

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Kristoffer Reitan wins the 2026 Truist Championship — first PGA Tour win in his 15th start.

The 28-year-old Norwegian shot 15-under 269 and edged Rickie Fowler and Hojgaard by two.

$3.6 million. And a whole lot of proof he belongs. #PGATour

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
What a final round at Quail Hollow.

Cameron Young shot 63 in R3 to charge. Rory McIlroy shot 67 in R4 to rebound. Alex Fitzpatrick had the lead.

And then Kristoffer Reitan went and won the whole thing. #PGATour

facebook.long_form:
A 28-year-old Norwegian came to Quail Hollow Club in Charlotte and left with the most significant win of his life.

Kristoffer Reitan won the 2026 Truist Championship on Sunday, shooting a final-round 2-under 69 to finish at 15-under 269 and beat Rickie Fowler and Nicolai Hojgaard by two shots. It was Reitan's first PGA Tour victory in his 15th career start, and he earned $3.6 million from the tournament's $20 million purse.

The final round was as dramatic as anyone could have hoped. Alex Fitzpatrick had led after Round 3 at 14-under. Cameron Young had fired a stunning 63 on Saturday to charge into contention at 12-under. Rory McIlroy, who had a dismal third-round 75 on Saturday, came roaring back with a final-round 67, showing the kind of form that will be useful heading into the PGA Championship at Aronimink this week. But none of them could match Reitan on the day that mattered most.

The Norwegian's story is one of persistence. He grinded his way through his first 14 PGA Tour starts without a victory before breaking through in Charlotte on one of the Tour's biggest regular-season stages. The win also secured his status on the Tour and placed his name among the first-time winners of 2026 alongside some notable company.

McIlroy finished approximately T19 after his final-round revival, heading to Aronimink with his long game in better shape than it looked on Saturday. The PGA Championship begins Thursday at Aronimink Golf Club in Pennsylvania.
facebook.image_caption: Kristoffer Reitan: 15-under, first PGA Tour win, $3.6M. The Norwegian rookie takes the 2026 Truist Championship at Quail Hollow. #PGATour

article.headline: Kristoffer Reitan Claims First PGA Tour Win at Truist Championship, Edges Fowler and Hojgaard by Two

stories.title: Lucas Herbert Wins LIV Virginia Wire-to-Wire — 4 Aces GC Takes Team Title

x_posts.label: Post 2
x_posts.text:
Lucas Herbert goes wire-to-wire at LIV Golf Virginia — fighting the flu the ENTIRE week.

Final score: 24-under, win by four.

Anthony Kim's bogey-free 62 helps 4 Aces GC force a team playoff — then win it. #LIVGolf

facebook.long_form:
Lucas Herbert had a simple goal heading into the final round at Trump National Golf Club in Sterling, Virginia: finish what he started. He'd led LIV Golf Virginia from the beginning, carried a four-shot cushion into Sunday, and had something working against him that most professional golfers would use as an excuse — a bout of flu that had persisted all week.

He didn't use it as an excuse. He played 3-under 69 on Sunday, extended his lead, and won the tournament at 24-under par, four shots clear of the field. It was Herbert's first LIV Golf individual title.

Sergio Garcia, who had been within striking distance on the back nine, made a move early in the final round but Herbert responded with a timely birdie to reassert control. Garcia settled for second place.

The team competition was more dramatic. The 4 Aces GC needed something extraordinary to force a playoff with the Fireballs, who were also at 49-under par as a team. What they got was Anthony Kim shooting a bogey-free 10-under 62 — one of the rounds of the LIV season — to level the standings. In the playoff, Kim and Thomas Detry made par on the 18th hole to clinch the team title.

It is the 4 Aces GC's third team title of the 2026 LIV Golf season, reinforcing their position as the circuit's dominant team.
facebook.image_caption: Wire-to-wire. Fighting the flu. 24-under. Lucas Herbert wins LIV Virginia and Anthony Kim's bogey-free 62 delivers the team title to 4 Aces GC. #LIVGolf

article.headline: Herbert Wins LIV Virginia Wire-to-Wire Despite Flu; Anthony Kim's Bogey-Free 62 Powers 4 Aces GC Team Win

stories.title: PGA Championship Preview — McIlroy's Grand Slam Bid, Scheffler Defends at Aronimink, May 14-17

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
The PGA Championship starts THURSDAY at Aronimink.

McIlroy: defending Masters champ, only player with a Grand Slam shot.
Scheffler: defending PGA champion, three runner-ups in 2026.
Young: two wins this year, no major yet.

May 14-17. #PGAChampionship

facebook.long_form:
The second major of 2026 is here, and the storylines could not be richer.

The PGA Championship begins Thursday at Aronimink Golf Club in Newtown Square, Pennsylvania — a Donald Ross masterpiece that the legendary architect himself described as "my masterpiece." The par-70 course stretches to 7,600 yards and features 75 bunkers, enough slopes, valleys, and doglegs to test every aspect of a player's game over 72 holes.

The centerpiece of the week is Rory McIlroy. The Northern Irishman successfully defended his Masters title in April, becoming the first player to retain the green jacket since Tiger Woods in 2002. That win made McIlroy the only player in 2026 with a genuine shot at the calendar Grand Slam — winning all four majors in one year. He has the Masters. The PGA Championship at Aronimink is next.

McIlroy arrives having bounced back at the Truist Championship with a final-round 67 after a rough Saturday. He is healthy, focused, and — by his own admission — more motivated than ever after the Masters defense. A second consecutive major would make 2026 arguably the greatest individual season the sport has seen in decades.

Standing in his way is defending champion Scottie Scheffler, who won last year's PGA by five shots and has spent 2026 in runner-up purgatory — three second-place finishes at the Masters, RBC Heritage, and Cadillac Championship. Scheffler skipped last week's Truist Championship specifically to prepare for Aronimink. He is ready.

Cameron Young, 29, arrives with two wins already in 2026 (the Players Championship and the Doral event) and no major championship on his resume. Young is precisely the kind of in-form, explosive player who could break through at Aronimink. Matt Fitzpatrick, the English former US Open champion, has three wins in his last four starts and brings a Ryder Cup veteran's course management to one of the most technical tracks in major championship golf.

LIV Golf players are also in the field. Bryson DeChambeau, Jon Rahm, and Dustin Johnson join their former PGA Tour colleagues for major championships — where the two tours briefly set aside their competition for four weeks a year.

The PGA Championship broadcasts May 14-17 on ESPN/ESPN+.
facebook.image_caption: McIlroy's Grand Slam bid. Scheffler defending. Cameron Young chasing his first major. The PGA Championship begins Thursday at Aronimink. #PGAChampionship

article.headline: PGA Championship 2026 Preview: McIlroy's Grand Slam Bid, Scheffler's Defense, and the Test of Aronimink

stories.title: Brandt Snedeker Wins Myrtle Beach Classic — First PGA Tour Win in Nearly 8 Years

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Brandt Snedeker just won the Myrtle Beach Classic.

His first PGA Tour win in nearly 8 years. His 10th career title.

And the win earns him a spot at the PGA Championship at Aronimink this week. Never count out the veterans. #PGATour

facebook.long_form:
Some stories in professional golf are about prodigies and breakout moments. This one is about patience.

Brandt Snedeker, 43 years old and nine-time PGA Tour winner heading into the week, closed with a 5-under 66 on Sunday at The Dunes Golf and Beach Club in Myrtle Beach, South Carolina, to win the 2026 ONEflight Myrtle Beach Classic at 18-under 266. It was his first PGA Tour victory in nearly eight years — and his 10th career title.

The wait between wins doesn't dull the feeling. Snedeker, who has been a staple of the PGA Tour for nearly two decades, showed on Sunday that his game still has the precision and the composure required to close out a professional golf tournament. Beau Hossler and Kevin Roy tied for third, two shots back, unable to match Snedeker's final-round charge.

The victory also earned Snedeker something he hadn't anticipated: a start in the PGA Championship at Aronimink this week. The Myrtle Beach Classic, like other recent PGA Tour events, offers an automatic berth in the following week's major for first-time qualifiers. Snedeker goes from celebrating in Myrtle Beach to teeing it up in Pennsylvania — a week he will remember for the rest of his career.

"Never count out the veterans" is the cliché that fits. This week, it earned its place.
facebook.image_caption: Brandt Snedeker: 10th career win, first in nearly 8 years. The Myrtle Beach Classic victory earns him a spot at the PGA Championship this week. #PGATour

article.headline: Brandt Snedeker Ends 8-Year Win Drought at Myrtle Beach Classic, Earns PGA Championship Spot

stories.title: Korda HOF Countdown — 4 Points Away as Kroger Queen City LPGA Begins This Week

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
Nelly Korda is 4 LPGA Hall of Fame points away (23 of 27 needed).

The Kroger Queen City Championship begins May 14 in Cincinnati.

Korda. Jeeno Thitikul. Top players in the world. Any given Sunday could be historic. #LPGA

facebook.long_form:
Nelly Korda is four points away from the LPGA Hall of Fame.

The 27-year-old American heads to Cincinnati this week for the Kroger Queen City Championship presented by P&G (May 14-17, Maketewah Country Club) with 23 of the required 27 points in her Hall of Fame ledger. She has added three LPGA wins in 2026 and has been the most dominant player on the tour across the past two-plus seasons.

The Hall of Fame points system values major championships and Player of the Year awards most highly, with each regular tour win adding to the total. At 23 points, Korda sits four away from the threshold — a milestone that could realistically arrive at any tournament she enters. She is the No. 2 player in the world rankings, just behind Jeeno Thitikul, who is also in the field at Queen City.

The tournament at Maketewah Country Club in Cincinnati features the top players in women's professional golf. Thitikul (No. 1) and Korda (No. 2) both committed early, along with Charley Hull, the popular English fan favorite, and Lydia Ko, the 2024 champion.

For Korda, every tournament now carries the weight of potential history. Four points is achievable in a single week — a win at Queen City, combined with the right combination of other results, could push her over the line. The countdown has been building all season. Cincinnati might be where it ends.
facebook.image_caption: 23 of 27. Nelly Korda needs 4 more Hall of Fame points. The Kroger Queen City Championship begins May 14 at Maketewah CC. History is possible every week. #LPGA

article.headline: Nelly Korda Is 4 Points From the LPGA Hall of Fame — The Kroger Queen City Championship Is Next
